Factory Machine StatusÂ¶

Name: Ng Yu Hang
Admin Number: [student-id]
Class: DAAA/FT/1B/07


Project ObjectiveÂ¶

Create a supervised classification model to predict Factory Machine Status

BackgroundÂ¶

This is a dataset collected from a manufacturing factory. The dataset contains various information on different machines. From the perspective of quality control, the factory wants to build a machine learning model to predict whether the machine is working properly or faulty


PhasesÂ¶

  1. Importing data (read csv file)
  2. Data Exploration, Data Preparation and Feature Engineering
  3. Setting up the models and model improvement tunings
  4. Training and Evaluating the improved models
  5. Creating a baseline model for performance comparison
  6. Determine Model Performance
  7. Reliability check
  8. Determining the best model for this task

Importing necessary librariesÂ¶

InÂ [1]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import warnings
from sklearn.dummy import DummyClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, StratifiedKFold, GridSearchCV, cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier, AdaBoostClassifier
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, accuracy_score, roc_curve, auc, f1_score, roc_auc_score
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, module="pandas")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, module="matplotlib")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, module="seaborn")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, module="numpy")
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, module="sklearn")

Here, we import all the required module libraries, as well as using warnings library to clear any warnings to make the report much more clean and neat.

Phase 1: Importing data (read csv file)Â¶

Importing the csv fileÂ¶

InÂ [2]:
data = pd.read_csv('factory_data.csv')
data.head(15)
Out[2]:
Unique ID Product ID Quality Ambient T (C) Process T (C) Rotation Speed (rpm) Torque (Nm) Tool Wear (min) Machine Status

1 2 Z62710 L 25.05 35.55 1408.0 46.3 3 0
2 3 T20224 L 24.95 35.35 1498.0 49.4 5 0
3 4 Z33024 L 25.05 35.45 1433.0 39.5 7 0

5 6 K53611 M 24.95 35.45 1425.0 41.9 11 0

12 13 T37320 M 25.45 35.95 1339.0 51.1 34 0

14 15 T39889 NaN 25.45 36.05 2035.0 19.6 40 0

Here, we import our csv file using pd.read_csv('factory_data.csv'), and using data.head(15) to show the first 15 rows of every column, just to have a general look at our dataset.

Phase 2: Data Exploration, Data Preparation and Feature EngineeringÂ¶

Describing our data contentÂ¶

InÂ [3]:
data.describe().style.background_gradient(cmap="Purples")
Out[3]:
Â  Unique ID Ambient T (C) Process T (C) Rotation Speed (rpm) Torque (Nm) Tool Wear (min) Machine Status
count 20000.000000 20000.000000 19600.000000 18812.000000 20000.000000 20000.000000 20000.000000
mean 10000.500000 26.904930 36.904291 1538.795397 39.986910 107.951000 0.033900
std 5773.647028 2.000834 1.483647 178.674963 9.968684 63.652555 0.180976
min 1.000000 22.150000 32.550000 1168.000000 3.800000 0.000000 0.000000
25% 5000.750000 25.250000 35.750000 1423.000000 33.200000 53.000000 0.000000
50% 10000.500000 26.950000 36.950000 1503.000000 40.100000 108.000000 0.000000
75% 15000.250000 28.450000 37.950000 1613.000000 46.800000 162.000000 0.000000
max 20000.000000 31.450000 40.750000 2886.000000 76.600000 253.000000 1.000000

The table shows that there are 20,000 rows, and 7 columns of numerical data. From the count row, we can see that there are missing numerical datas for Rotation Speed and Process T (C). However we are unsure if other non-numerical data columns have missing data, so we need data.isnull().sum() to confirm, as well as view the number of missing rows easier.

Further check for number of missing datasÂ¶

InÂ [4]:
data.isnull().sum()
Out[4]:
Unique ID                  0
Product ID                 0
Quality                  991
Ambient T (C)              0
Process T (C)            400
Rotation Speed (rpm)    1188
Torque (Nm)                0
Tool Wear (min)            0
Machine Status             0
dtype: int64

From this, we could see there are 991 missing values for Quality, 400 missing values for Process T (C) and 1188 missing values for Rotation Speed (rpm). Our models need complete datas to train, hence we can either impute using imputing methods like SimpleImputer() or we can drop (remove) the rows with missing data, as we have enough data to train a reliable model with.

Visualization of % of missing datas:Â¶

InÂ [5]:
data.isna().mean().plot.barh(figsize=(10,10))
plt.ylabel("Data Columns")
plt.xlabel("Percentage of missing values (%)")
plt.show()

Setting up Data for both dropna() and SimpleImputer()Â¶

InÂ [6]:
imputed_data = data.copy() 
dropna_data = data.dropna() 
print("Number of rows after dropping missing values:", dropna_data.shape[0])

Number of rows after dropping missing values: 17529

This tells us that we now have 17,529 rows of data with completely filled original data.

For the imputed_date, we copied the original data instead of tampering with the original data, so that we can refer back to it afterwards if needed for any referencing.

There are pros and cons of using both either dropna() method or SimpleImputer() method. With the main ones as follows:


dropna() pros is that it removes the rows with missing columns, so there is no need to guess fake data, hence every data left in the dataset is completely true.

dropna() cons is that because it is removing rows of data, we would end up with lesser data to work with. Which can be a problem for smaller datasets, or if there is a LARGE amount of missing data.

SimpleImputer() pros is that we get to retain the amount of data in our dataset, with datas that are as close to being correct as possible using the other available datas.

SimpleImputer() cons is that while we get to keep the amount of data, the accuracy of the model might suffer because of the fake imputed data.

Selecting data features and targetÂ¶

Selecting the target columnÂ¶

InÂ [7]:
target_column = 'Machine Status'

X_dropna = dropna_data.drop(target_column, axis=1)
y_dropna = dropna_data[target_column]

X_imputed = imputed_data.drop(target_column, axis=1)
y_imputed = imputed_data[target_column]

I create a variable called target_column so we can change the target column conveniently while not having to change the rest of the code that uses the target column code.

I then assign y to the target column so that y contains the target data.


I drop the target column from the original data so that the target column does not get used for to train the model, preventing a data leakage, making sure the model learns it using the patterns in the features, rather than being able to refer to the answer directly.

Pick feature data and identify the columns with numerical and categorical dataÂ¶

InÂ [8]:
numerical_columns = ['Ambient T (C)', 'Process T (C)', 'Rotation Speed (rpm)', 'Torque (Nm)', 'Tool Wear (min)']
categorical_columns = ['Quality']

I first picked out the features I want to include and assign them to numerical_columns and categorical_columns according to whether they contain numerical or categorical data. The excluded columns (Unique ID and Product ID) are columns that contain irrelevant data, hence we do not need to include them as we will not be using them to train the model.

AnalysisÂ¶

Correlation AnalysisÂ¶

Using a correlation matrix, it helps us analyze the strength of the relationship between two or more features.

InÂ [9]:
plt.figure(figsize=(13, 8))
sns.heatmap(data[numerical_columns].join(data[[target_column]]).corr(), annot=True, cmap="PuBu", linewidths=0.5)
plt.title("Correlation Matrix with Target Column 'Machine Status'")
plt.show()

From this, we could see that the Torque (Nm) has the highest correlation to the target variable

Univariate AnalysisÂ¶

HistogramsÂ¶

We can explore the distribution of each of the variables using histograms to visualize how frequent each range of values occurs.

InÂ [10]:
data[numerical_columns].hist(figsize=(14,14))
plt.show()
Box PlotsÂ¶

Using a box plot, we can visualize each numerical variable's distribution and their spread, as well as their outliers.

InÂ [11]:
data[numerical_columns].boxplot(figsize=(14,7))
plt.show()

Notice that Rotation Speed has a lot of outliers. Even though they are outliers, they are useful for models to learn what are the outcomes of the target data in more extreme conditions (the outlier conditions). This can help with greater reliability of the models.

Checking for class imbalance in target dataÂ¶

InÂ [12]:
data[target_column].value_counts().plot.pie(autopct="%.2f")
Out[12]:
<Axes: ylabel='count'>

From this pie chart, we can see that there is a massive class imbalance in the target column. There are 96.61% instances of "0" and only 3.39% instance of "1" appearing.

Hence, what we could expect is a very large difference in accuracy between predicting the two results.

Feature Engineering: Preprocessing our dataÂ¶

Applying the preprocessors to both dropna() data and SimpleImputer() datasÂ¶

InÂ [13]:
# Preprocessor for dropna data
dropna_preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', StandardScaler(), numerical_columns),
    ('cat', OneHotEncoder(drop='first'), categorical_columns)
])

# Preprocessor for imputed data with SimpleImputer
imputer_preprocessor = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', Pipeline([('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='mean')), ('scaler', StandardScaler())]), numerical_columns),
    ('cat', Pipeline([('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')), ('encoder', OneHotEncoder(drop='first'))]), categorical_columns)
])

Here, i assign column transformers (StandardScaler and OneHotEncoder) to both dropna data and imputed data.

StandardScaler scales numerical features to have a mean of 0, and a standard deviation of 1. This helps with improving many models' performance.

OneHotEncoder is used on categorical data to create numerical data by creating binary columns.

(drop='first') is used for dropping the first column in the OneHotEncoder binary column to create redundancy (for instance, if there are 3 categories, if 2 columns of the categories are 0, it just means its the 3rd category)

Train_test_split for both dropna() and SimpleImputer() dataÂ¶

I use train_test_split to split the data into training and testing data in the ratio of the value in test_size. I used 0.3, which means it would split the data to have 70% data for training, 30% data for testing.

InÂ [14]:
X_train_dropna, X_test_dropna, y_train_dropna, y_test_dropna = train_test_split(X_dropna, y_dropna, test_size=0.3, stratify=y_dropna)
X_train_imputed, X_test_imputed, y_train_imputed, y_test_imputed = train_test_split(X_imputed, y_imputed, test_size=0.3, stratify=y_imputed)

I used two different train_test_splits so that each data wouldn't affect each other after imputation or dropping, so I can have a fair comparison between their performance and reliability.

Phase 3: Setting up the models and model improvement tuningsÂ¶

Setting up KFoldsÂ¶

I am using Stratified K-Fold instead of the normal KFold because as seen in above analysis, there is a massive class imbalance. Stratified K-Fold is well-suited for classification tasks as it maintains the proportion of each class in each fold.

This results in better model consistency as well as lower biasness which produces a more reliable model.

InÂ [15]:
stratified_kf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

Defining parameter grids for modelsÂ¶

Parameter Grids are used for controlling the model's complexity, regularization and behaviour. They define the range of hyperparameters that will be tested during hyperparameter tuning. They are then used with GridSearchCV to find the best combination of parameters and the best config for the model.

InÂ [16]:
param_grids = {
    'Logistic Regression': {
        'logreg__C': [0.0001, 0.001, 0.01, 0.05],
        'logreg__penalty': ['l2'],
        'logreg__solver': ['liblinear'],
        'logreg__class_weight': ['balanced']
    },
    'SVC': {
        'svc__C': [0.001, 0.005, 0.01],
        'svc__kernel': ['linear'],
        'svc__gamma': ['scale'],
        'svc__class_weight': ['balanced']
    },
    'Random Forest': {
        'forest__n_estimators': [50, 100, 150],
        'forest__max_depth': [5, 10, 15],
        'forest__min_samples_split': [40, 50, 60],
        'forest__class_weight': ['balanced']
    },
    'KNN': {
        'knn__n_neighbors': [3, 5, 7],
        'knn__weights': ['uniform']
    },
    'Decision Tree': {
        'dt__max_depth': [5, 10, 15],
        'dt__min_samples_split': [40, 50, 60],
        'dt__class_weight': ['balanced']
    },
    'Gradient Boosting': {
        'gb__n_estimators': [50, 100, 150],
        'gb__learning_rate': [0.01, 0.02, 0.03],
        'gb__max_depth': [3, 4, 5]
    },
    'AdaBoost': {
        'ab__n_estimators': [50, 100, 150],
        'ab__learning_rate': [0.01, 0.02, 0.03]
    }
}

As the hyperparameters I have used show, I have leaned more towards underfitting the models so it's less bias towards the majority data. Since this is an imbalanced dataset, it is much safer to underfit.

This is because, even though underfitting can cause a drop in performance, it does not overfit to generalize the majority data, it instead gives a more reliable result that fairly determines both the majority and minority data.

Defining Models with pipelines for both dropna and simpleimputer dataÂ¶

This defines the models for dropna() dataÂ¶

InÂ [17]:
models_dropna = {
    'Logistic Regression': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('logreg', LogisticRegression(max_iter=500, class_weight='balanced'))
    ]),
    'SVC': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('svc', SVC(probability=True, class_weight='balanced'))
    ]),
    'Random Forest': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('forest', RandomForestClassifier())
    ]),
    'KNN': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('knn', KNeighborsClassifier())
    ]),
    'Decision Tree': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('dt', DecisionTreeClassifier())
    ]),
    'Gradient Boosting': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('gb', GradientBoostingClassifier())
    ]),
    'AdaBoost': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', dropna_preprocessor),
        ('ab', AdaBoostClassifier())
    ])
}

This defines the models for SimpleImputer() dataÂ¶

InÂ [18]:
models_imputed = {
    'Logistic Regression': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('logreg', LogisticRegression(max_iter=500, class_weight='balanced'))
    ]),
    'SVC': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('svc', SVC(probability=True, class_weight='balanced'))
    ]),
    'Random Forest': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('forest', RandomForestClassifier())
    ]),
    'KNN': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('knn', KNeighborsClassifier())
    ]),
    'Decision Tree': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('dt', DecisionTreeClassifier())
    ]),
    'Gradient Boosting': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('gb', GradientBoostingClassifier())
    ]),
    'AdaBoost': Pipeline([
        ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
        ('ab', AdaBoostClassifier())
    ])
}

Using pipeline, I can link the models to use the respective data processing methods using the previously defined preprocessors. Pipelines make the code cleaner and easier to make changes to, as well as make sure the same transformations are consistently applied to both train and test data, preventing Data Leakage.

Testing how well the models run without the hyperparameters firstÂ¶

InÂ [19]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

for name, model in models_dropna.items():
    model.fit(X_train_dropna, y_train_dropna)
    y_pred = model.predict(X_test_dropna)
    y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test_dropna)[:, 1]
    accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test_dropna, y_pred)
    f1 = f1_score(y_test_dropna, y_pred, average='weighted')
    fpr, tpr, _ = roc_curve(y_test_dropna, y_prob)
    auc_score = roc_auc_score(y_test_dropna, y_prob)
    
    print(f'Model {name} prediction accuracy is {accuracy*100:.1f}%, f1-score is {f1*100:.1f}%, AUC score is {auc_score:.3f}')
    plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{name} (AUC = {auc_score:.3f})')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], linestyle='--', color='grey')
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve')
plt.legend(loc='lower right')
plt.show()

Model Logistic Regression prediction accuracy is 80.6%, f1-score is 86.7%, AUC score is 0.904
Model SVC prediction accuracy is 92.4%, f1-score is 94.2%, AUC score is 0.973
Model Random Forest prediction accuracy is 99.3%, f1-score is 99.2%, AUC score is 0.989
Model KNN prediction accuracy is 97.9%, f1-score is 97.7%, AUC score is 0.944
Model Decision Tree prediction accuracy is 99.1%, f1-score is 99.1%, AUC score is 0.928
Model Gradient Boosting prediction accuracy is 98.7%, f1-score is 98.6%, AUC score is 0.975
Model AdaBoost prediction accuracy is 97.4%, f1-score is 97.2%, AUC score is 0.965

The models seem to already do well without hyperparameters, the ROC curve shows that they are performing oddly well, with many of the graphs having a very right angled curve at the top, which proves that its not actually good at predicting each class well.

We can also see that models like KNN and Decision Tree seem to not be doing well as they have a very sharp turn.

Feature ImportanceÂ¶

InÂ [20]:
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, message="The SAMME.R algorithm")

# Process models trained on dropna data
for model_name, pipeline in models_dropna.items():
    pipeline.fit(X_train_dropna, y_train_dropna)

    preprocessor = pipeline.named_steps['preprocessor']
    model_step_name = list(pipeline.named_steps.keys())[-1]
    model = pipeline.named_steps[model_step_name]

    if hasattr(model, 'feature_importances_'):
        print(f"Model: {model_name}")
        feature_importances = model.feature_importances_
        feature_names = preprocessor.get_feature_names_out()
        feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
            'Feature': feature_names,
            'Importance': feature_importances
        }).sort_values(by='Importance', ascending=True)

        # Plot feature importances
        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        sns.barplot(x='Importance', y='Feature', data=feature_importance_df)
        plt.title(f'{model_name} Feature Importances (Dropna Data)')
        plt.show()

# Process models trained on imputed data
for model_name, pipeline in models_imputed.items():
    pipeline.fit(X_train_imputed, y_train_imputed)

    preprocessor = pipeline.named_steps['preprocessor']
    model_step_name = list(pipeline.named_steps.keys())[-1]
    model = pipeline.named_steps[model_step_name]

    if hasattr(model, 'feature_importances_'):
        print(f"Model: {model_name}")
        feature_importances = model.feature_importances_
        feature_names = preprocessor.get_feature_names_out()
        feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
            'Feature': feature_names,
            'Importance': feature_importances
        }).sort_values(by='Importance', ascending=True)

        plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
        sns.barplot(x='Importance', y='Feature', data=feature_importance_df)
        plt.title(f'{model_name} Feature Importances (Imputed Data)')
        plt.show()

Model: Random Forest

Model: Decision Tree

Model: Gradient Boosting

Model: AdaBoost

Model: Random Forest

Model: Decision Tree

Model: Gradient Boosting

Model: AdaBoost

The bar graphs above show the feature importances for the models that support it (i.e. Only models with feature_importances_ would work) I will not be removing any features since they seem to be mostly somewhat important. I will also not be feature engineering new features since they already have quite high predictive power, and because this is an imbalanced date, featuring new features mighit cause it to be even more biased towards majority data.

Phase 4: Training and Evaluating the improved ModelsÂ¶

Using a for loop to train every model with their respective pipelineÂ¶

Using a for loop, I run every model in the pipeline using GridSearchCV

GridSearchCV is a tool that automates the tuning of hyperparameters for machine learning models. It reads the hyperparameters defined in param_grids[model_name], and stratified_kf defined earlier, to "fold" each hyperparameter combination and evaluate them using cross validation. GridSearchCV then fits the training data into the model with the best hyperparameter that produced the best performance.

I then insert the best score and the best model into the results

InÂ [21]:
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning, message="The SAMME.R algorithm")

# Training and evaluating models with Drop Missing Data approach
dropna_results = {}
for model_name, pipeline in models_dropna.items():
    grid = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid=param_grids[model_name], cv=stratified_kf, scoring='roc_auc', verbose=3)
    grid.fit(X_train_dropna, y_train_dropna)
    dropna_results[model_name] = {
        'best_score': grid.best_score_,
        'best_estimator': grid.best_estimator_
    }

# Training and evaluating models with Impute Missing Data approach
imputed_results = {}
for model_name, pipeline in models_imputed.items():
    grid = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid=param_grids[model_name], cv=stratified_kf, scoring='roc_auc', verbose=3)
    grid.fit(X_train_imputed, y_train_imputed)
    imputed_results[model_name] = {
        'best_score': grid.best_score_,
        'best_estimator': grid.best_estimator_
    }

Fitting 5 folds for each of 4 candidates, totalling 20 fits
[CV 1/5] END logreg__C=0.0001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.828 total time=   0.0s
[CV 2/5] END logreg__C=0.0001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.811 total time=   0.0s
[CV 3/5] END logreg__C=0.0001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.799 total time=   0.0s
[CV 4/5] END logreg__C=0.0001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.777 total time=   0.0s
[CV 5/5] END logreg__C=0.0001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.835 total time=   0.0s
[CV 1/5] END logreg__C=0.001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.881 total time=   0.0s
[CV 2/5] END logreg__C=0.001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.853 total time=   0.0s
[CV 3/5] END logreg__C=0.001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.849 total time=   0.0s
[CV 4/5] END logreg__C=0.001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.872 total time=   0.0s
[CV 5/5] END logreg__C=0.001, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.876 total time=   0.0s
[CV 1/5] END logreg__C=0.01, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.908 total time=   0.0s
[CV 2/5] END logreg__C=0.01, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.891 total time=   0.0s
[CV 3/5] END logreg__C=0.01, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.858 total time=   0.0s
[CV 4/5] END logreg__C=0.01, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.920 total time=   0.0s
[CV 5/5] END logreg__C=0.01, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.887 total time=   0.0s
[CV 1/5] END logreg__C=0.05, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.911 total time=   0.0s
[CV 2/5] END logreg__C=0.05, logreg__class_weight=balanced, logreg__penalty=l2, logreg__solver=liblinear;, score=0.895 total time=   0.0s

As seen from the output, I am outputting every model fit. This is done using the verbose=(number) method. Verbose outputs the folding process happening, which is a good visualization to show whether your program has froze or is just taking some time to run.

Phase 5: Creating a baseline model for performance comparisonÂ¶

InÂ [22]:
dummyevaluation = []


dummy_pipeline_imputed = Pipeline([
    ('preprocessor', imputer_preprocessor),
    ('dummy', DummyClassifier(strategy='stratified'))
])


dummy_pipeline_imputed.fit(X_train_imputed, y_train_imputed)
y_pred_dummy_imputed = dummy_pipeline_imputed.predict(X_test_imputed)
y_pred_proba_dummy_imputed = dummy_pipeline_imputed.predict_proba(X_test_imputed)[:, 1]  


dummy_test_accuracy_imputed = accuracy_score(y_test_imputed, y_pred_dummy_imputed)
dummy_f1_imputed = f1_score(y_test_imputed, y_pred_dummy_imputed, average='weighted', zero_division=1)


dummyevaluation.append({
    'Approach': 'Impute Missing Data',
    'Model': 'Dummy Baseline (Stratified)',
    'Test Set Accuracy': dummy_test_accuracy_imputed,
    'Test F1 Score': dummy_f1_imputed
})


print("Classification Report for Imputed Data (Stratified):")
print(classification_report(y_test_imputed, y_pred_dummy_imputed, zero_division=1))


dummy_pipeline_dropna = Pipeline([
    ('dummy', DummyClassifier(strategy='stratified'))
])


dummy_pipeline_dropna.fit(X_train_dropna, y_train_dropna)
y_pred_dummy_dropna = dummy_pipeline_dropna.predict(X_test_dropna)
y_pred_proba_dummy_dropna = dummy_pipeline_dropna.predict_proba(X_test_dropna)[:, 1]  # Probability for positive class


dummy_test_accuracy_dropna = accuracy_score(y_test_dropna, y_pred_dummy_dropna)
dummy_f1_dropna = f1_score(y_test_dropna, y_pred_dummy_dropna, average='weighted', zero_division=1)


dummyevaluation.append({
    'Approach': 'Drop Missing Data',
    'Model': 'Dummy Baseline (Stratified)',
    'Test Set Accuracy': dummy_test_accuracy_dropna,
    'Test F1 Score': dummy_f1_dropna
})


print("\nClassification Report for Dropna Data (Stratified):")
print(classification_report(y_test_dropna, y_pred_dummy_dropna, zero_division=1))


dummyevaluation_df = pd.DataFrame(dummyevaluation).set_index('Model')
print("\nModel Evaluation Summary:")
print(dummyevaluation_df)


fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed, _ = roc_curve(y_test_imputed, y_pred_proba_dummy_imputed)
roc_auc_imputed = auc(fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed)

fpr_dropna, tpr_dropna, _ = roc_curve(y_test_dropna, y_pred_proba_dummy_dropna)
roc_auc_dropna = auc(fpr_dropna, tpr_dropna)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed, label=f'Stratified Dummy (Imputed) ROC curve (area = {roc_auc_imputed:.2f})')
plt.plot(fpr_dropna, tpr_dropna, linestyle='--', label=f'Stratified Dummy (Dropna) ROC curve (area = {roc_auc_dropna:.2f})')

# Plot the ROC settings
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--')
plt.xlim([0.0, 1.0])
plt.ylim([0.0, 1.05])
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curves for Dummy Classifiers (Stratified)')
plt.legend(loc="lower right")
plt.show()

Classification Report for Imputed Data (Stratified):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.97      0.97      0.97      5797
           1       0.01      0.01      0.01       203

    accuracy                           0.93      6000
   macro avg       0.49      0.49      0.49      6000
weighted avg       0.93      0.93      0.93      6000


Classification Report for Dropna Data (Stratified):
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0       0.97      0.96      0.97      5081
           1       0.03      0.03      0.03       178

    accuracy                           0.93      5259
   macro avg       0.50      0.50      0.50      5259
weighted avg       0.93      0.93      0.93      5259


Model Evaluation Summary:
                                        Approach  Test Set Accuracy  \
Model                                                                 
Dummy Baseline (Stratified)  Impute Missing Data           0.933333   
Dummy Baseline (Stratified)    Drop Missing Data           0.932497   

                             Test F1 Score  
Model                                       
Dummy Baseline (Stratified)       0.933174  
Dummy Baseline (Stratified)       0.933299

Even though from the results, we can see that the test set accuracy is very high, however the ROC curve show that the model is achieving that from pure guessing. This shows that this model is not reliable. As expected from an imbalanced dataset

We will use this model as baseline to making a good model that can reliably predict data by learning patterns, rather than guessing (i.e. a higher ROC AUC score)

Phase 6: Determine Model PerformanceÂ¶

Creating a new array for the results to append into.

InÂ [23]:
evaluation_results = []

Collating model performance for dropna() datasÂ¶

InÂ [24]:
approach = 'Drop Missing Data'
results = dropna_results
X_test = X_test_dropna
y_test = y_test_dropna

for model_name, result in results.items():
    best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
    
    y_pred = best_pipeline.predict(X_test)

    accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    train_F1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)
    test_F1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)

    evaluation_results.append({
        'Approach': approach,
        'Model': model_name,
        'Test Set Accuracy': accuracy,
        'Training F1 Score': train_F1,
        'Test F1 Score': test_F1
    })

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)

Collating model performance data for SimpleImputer() datasÂ¶

InÂ [25]:
approach = 'Impute Missing Data'
results = imputed_results
X_test = X_test_imputed
y_test = y_test_imputed

for model_name, result in results.items():
    best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
    
    y_pred = best_pipeline.predict(X_test)

    accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
    train_F1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)
    test_F1 = f1_score(y_test, y_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)

    evaluation_results.append({
        'Approach': approach,
        'Model': model_name,
        'Test Set Accuracy': accuracy,
        'Training F1 Score': train_F1,
        'Test F1 Score': test_F1
    })

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)

Display the evaluation resultsÂ¶

InÂ [26]:
evaluation_df = pd.DataFrame(evaluation_results)
evaluation_df.set_index('Model', inplace=True)

evaluation_df
Out[26]:
Approach Test Set Accuracy Training F1 Score Test F1 Score
Model
Logistic Regression Drop Missing Data 0.804906 0.866033 0.866033
SVC Drop Missing Data 0.813843 0.871769 0.871769
Random Forest Drop Missing Data 0.966724 0.971298 0.971298
KNN Drop Missing Data 0.977372 0.973809 0.973809
Decision Tree Drop Missing Data 0.927363 0.943983 0.943983
Gradient Boosting Drop Missing Data 0.989352 0.988699 0.988699
AdaBoost Drop Missing Data 0.968245 0.954472 0.954472
Logistic Regression Impute Missing Data 0.811000 0.869851 0.869851
SVC Impute Missing Data 0.826500 0.879704 0.879704
Random Forest Impute Missing Data 0.966167 0.970850 0.970850
KNN Impute Missing Data 0.975833 0.971727 0.971727
Decision Tree Impute Missing Data 0.910667 0.933340 0.933340
Gradient Boosting Impute Missing Data 0.987167 0.986108 0.986108
AdaBoost Impute Missing Data 0.968500 0.955035 0.955035

This shows us each model performance for each approach with their respective Test Set Accuracy, Training F1 Score and Test F1 Score

Plotting the comparison resultsÂ¶

InÂ [27]:
# Plotting the comparison results
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.barplot(x=evaluation_df.index, y='Test Set Accuracy', hue='Approach', data=evaluation_df)
plt.title('Comparison of Test Set Accuracy - Drop Missing Data vs Impute Missing Data')
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.show()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.barplot(x=evaluation_df.index, y='Test F1 Score', hue='Approach', data=evaluation_df)
plt.title('Comparison of Test F1 Score - Drop Missing Data vs Impute Missing Data')
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.show()

Classification report and confusion matrix for each model to evaluate class-based performanceÂ¶

InÂ [28]:
datasets = {
    "Drop Missing Data": {"X_test": X_test_dropna, "y_test": y_test_dropna, "results": dropna_results},
    "Impute Missing Data": {"X_test": X_test_imputed, "y_test": y_test_imputed, "results": imputed_results},
}


for approach, data in datasets.items():
    X_test = data["X_test"]
    y_test = data["y_test"]
    results = data["results"]

    print(f"\n======= Evaluation for {approach} =======")
    
    for model_name, result in results.items():

        best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
        y_pred = best_pipeline.predict(X_test)
        

        print(f"\nClassification Report for {model_name} ({approach}):")
        print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["Class 0", "Class 1"]))
        

        conf_matrix = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
        plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
        sns.heatmap(conf_matrix, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues', xticklabels=["Class 0", "Class 1"], yticklabels=["Class 0", "Class 1"])
        plt.title(f"Confusion Matrix for {model_name} ({approach})")
        plt.xlabel("Predicted Class")
        plt.ylabel("True Class")
        plt.show()

----- Evaluation for Drop Missing Data -----

weighted avg       0.97      0.97      0.96      6000

From this, we can see clearly the performance of the models for predicting EACH class 0 and 1.

We want to look for models who have F1 Scores near 1.00 for both Class 0 and Class 1. Which seems to be Gradient Boosting

Comparing the results of dropping or imputing missing dataÂ¶

InÂ [29]:
dropna_accuracies = [result['Test Set Accuracy'] for result in evaluation_results if result['Approach'] == 'Drop Missing Data']
impute_accuracies = [result['Test Set Accuracy'] for result in evaluation_results if result['Approach'] == 'Impute Missing Data']

mean_accuracy_dropna = np.mean(dropna_accuracies)
mean_accuracy_impute = np.mean(impute_accuracies)

print(f"Average Test Set Accuracy with Drop Missing Data: {mean_accuracy_dropna:.4f}")
print(f"Average Test Set Accuracy with Impute Missing Data: {mean_accuracy_impute:.4f}")

if mean_accuracy_dropna > mean_accuracy_impute:
    print("\nConclusion: Dropping missing data resulted in higher overall accuracy.")
elif mean_accuracy_dropna < mean_accuracy_impute:
    print("\nConclusion: Imputing missing data resulted in higher overall accuracy.")
else:
    print("\nConclusion: Both approaches resulted in the same overall accuracy.")

Average Test Set Accuracy with Drop Missing Data: 0.9211
Average Test Set Accuracy with Impute Missing Data: 0.9208

Conclusion: Dropping missing data resulted in higher overall accuracy.

From this checker, we can see that even though one type of data has higher accuracy than the other, the difference is minimal.

My explanation for this is because imputing data is essentially filling up missing datas with fake data that they guessed from using the other datas, while this can be useful for smaller datas as it can keep our data size for better predictions, for larger datas, we can afford the luxury of dropping the missing datas to leave out the most truthful and realistic datas.

Hence, in conclusion, dropna data and imputed data has minimal difference in THIS dataset.

Phase 7: Reliability CheckÂ¶

Checking for overfitting with ROC curveÂ¶

InÂ [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))
for model_name, result in results.items():
    best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
    if hasattr(best_pipeline, 'predict_proba'):
        y_prob = best_pipeline.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
        fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)
        roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)
        
        plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{model_name} (AUC = {roc_auc:.2f})')

plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', label='Chance Line')
plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('ROC Curve for All Models')
plt.legend(loc='lower right')
plt.show()

From the ROC curve, it shows the models all have very high accuracy, while Logistic Regression has a much much lower accuracy than the rest, most of the models are extremely close to 100% ROC Score. This could be a result of overfitting, hence we need to check if the models are overfitting to make sure their predictive abilities are indeed reliable.

Checking for overfitting by comparing Train Data Accuracy with Test Data AccuracyÂ¶

This will compare the train scores with the test scores, if there is a 0.1 difference and above, it might be overfitting or underfitting, if it doesn't, it's well-fitted model.

InÂ [31]:
overfitting_results = []

for model_name, result in results.items():
    best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
    
    y_train_pred = best_pipeline.predict(X_train_imputed)
    y_test_pred = best_pipeline.predict(X_test_imputed)
    
    train_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_train_imputed, y_train_pred)
    test_accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test_imputed, y_test_pred)

    train_F1 = f1_score(y_train_imputed, y_train_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)
    test_F1 = f1_score(y_test_imputed, y_test_pred, average='weighted', zero_division=1)

    overfitting_results.append({
        'Model': model_name,
        'Training Set Accuracy': train_accuracy,
        'Test Set Accuracy': test_accuracy,
        'Training F1 Score': train_F1,
        'Test F1 Score': test_F1
    })
    
    print(f'--- {model_name} ---')
    print(f'Training Set Accuracy: {train_accuracy:.4f}')
    print(f'Test Set Accuracy: {test_accuracy:.4f}')
    print(f'Training F1 Score: {train_F1:.4f}')
    print(f'Test F1 Score: {test_F1:.4f}')
    
    if train_accuracy > test_accuracy + 0.1: 
        print("Result: This model is likely overfitting.")
    elif abs(train_accuracy - test_accuracy) <= 0.1:
        print("Result: This model is well-fitted.")
    else:
        print("Result: This model might be underfitting.")
    print()

overfitting_df = pd.DataFrame(overfitting_results).set_index('Model')
print("\nOverfitting Test Summary:")
print(overfitting_df)

# Visualize the training vs. test set performance
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.bar(overfitting_df.index, overfitting_df['Training Set Accuracy'], label='Training Set Accuracy', alpha=0.6)
plt.bar(overfitting_df.index, overfitting_df['Test Set Accuracy'], label='Test Set Accuracy', alpha=0.6)
plt.title('Training vs. Test Set Accuracy for Each Model')
plt.xlabel('Model')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.legend()
plt.xticks(rotation=35, ha="right")
plt.show()

--- Logistic Regression ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.8111
Test Set Accuracy: 0.8110
Training F1 Score: 0.8698
Test F1 Score: 0.8699
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- SVC ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.8274
Test Set Accuracy: 0.8265
Training F1 Score: 0.8801
Test F1 Score: 0.8797
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- Random Forest ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.9737
Test Set Accuracy: 0.9662
Training F1 Score: 0.9771
Test F1 Score: 0.9708
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- KNN ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.9809
Test Set Accuracy: 0.9758
Training F1 Score: 0.9779
Test F1 Score: 0.9717
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- Decision Tree ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.9191
Test Set Accuracy: 0.9107
Training F1 Score: 0.9392
Test F1 Score: 0.9333
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- Gradient Boosting ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.9925
Test Set Accuracy: 0.9872
Training F1 Score: 0.9921
Test F1 Score: 0.9861
Result: This model is well-fitted.

--- AdaBoost ---
Training Set Accuracy: 0.9674
Test Set Accuracy: 0.9685
Training F1 Score: 0.9527
Test F1 Score: 0.9550
Result: This model is well-fitted.


Overfitting Test Summary:
                     Training Set Accuracy  Test Set Accuracy  \
Model                                                           
Logistic Regression               0.811071           0.811000   
SVC                               0.827357           0.826500   
Random Forest                     0.973714           0.966167   
KNN                               0.980857           0.975833   
Decision Tree                     0.919143           0.910667   
Gradient Boosting                 0.992500           0.987167   
AdaBoost                          0.967429           0.968500   

                     Training F1 Score  Test F1 Score  
Model                                                  
Logistic Regression           0.869779       0.869851  
SVC                           0.880096       0.879704  
Random Forest                 0.977094       0.970850  
KNN                           0.977949       0.971727  
Decision Tree                 0.939182       0.933340  
Gradient Boosting             0.992077       0.986108  
AdaBoost                      0.952666       0.955035

Here, we compare the accuracy of Training Results with the Test Results. If overfitting is happening, there would be significant differences between them, hence I will set a maximum difference of 0.1 for it to be considered well-fitted.

From the results, we can see that all the models are well-fitted. Indicating no overfitting or underfitting, telling us that the models are reliable.

Comparing our model result to the dummy model resultÂ¶

Model AccuracyÂ¶

How good are our models compared to the dummy model?

InÂ [32]:
evaluation_df_reset = evaluation_df.reset_index()
evaluation_df_reset = evaluation_df_reset[['Model', 'Test Set Accuracy']]


dummy_accuracy_row = pd.DataFrame([{'Model': 'Dummy Baseline (Stratified - Imputed)', 'Test Set Accuracy': dummy_test_accuracy_imputed}])
comparison_df_combined = pd.concat([evaluation_df_reset, dummy_accuracy_row], ignore_index=True)

comparison_df_combined = comparison_df_combined.sort_values(by='Test Set Accuracy', ascending=True)


plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))
sns.barplot(x='Test Set Accuracy', y='Model', data=comparison_df_combined, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Model Test Set Accuracy Comparison with Dummy Baseline (Imputed)')
plt.xlabel('Test Set Accuracy')
plt.ylabel('Model')
plt.xlim(0, 1)
plt.show()

Model Discriminative PowerÂ¶

It has good accuracy, but are the models more capable of discriminating results compared to the baseline?

InÂ [33]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))


for model_name, result in results.items():
    best_pipeline = result['best_estimator']
    if hasattr(best_pipeline, 'predict_proba'):
        y_prob = best_pipeline.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
        fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)
        roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)
        plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f'{model_name} (AUC = {roc_auc:.2f})', linewidth=1.5)


fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed, _ = roc_curve(y_test_imputed, y_pred_proba_dummy_imputed)
roc_auc_imputed = auc(fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed)
plt.plot(fpr_imputed, tpr_imputed, linestyle='--', label=f'Stratified Dummy (Imputed) (AUC = {roc_auc_imputed:.2f})', linewidth=1.5)


plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], 'k--', label='Chance Line')


plt.xlabel('False Positive Rate')
plt.ylabel('True Positive Rate')
plt.title('Combined ROC Curves for Models and Dummy Classifiers')
plt.legend(loc='lower right')
plt.show()

As we can see, we have greatly increased the discriminative power of the actual models compared to the dummy baseline model which was randomly guessing, as well as the models without hyperparameter tuning. This shows that our models are much more reliable. Looking back at the pie chart showing that there are 96.61% of data which is "0", our models are mostly performing around that accuracy with a good ROC curve, signifying real-world reliability.

Final Phase: Determining the best model for predicting Factory Machine StatusÂ¶

Even tho we have found the model with the highest pure test accuracy is Gradient Boosting, we need to also compare the F1 Scores to determine the best model that suits this project.

The reason why, is because there is severe class imbalance in our datafile, hence the need to use F1 Score comparison instead of using our normal test accuracy data, as the result would be dominated by the majority class. For instance, we have 96.61% of our data as "0", hence if the model purely guesses everything as "0", it technically has a 96.61% accuracy, which is very misleading.

InÂ [34]:
evaluation_df = evaluation_df.sort_values(by='Test F1 Score', ascending=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
sns.barplot(x=evaluation_df.index, y='Test F1 Score', hue='Approach', data=evaluation_df)
plt.title('Comparison of Test F1 Score - Drop Missing Data vs Impute Missing Data')
plt.ylim(0, 1)
plt.show()

According to the bar graph, which was set to be sorted by ascending, Gradient Boosting remains the best classification model for this, when comparing the F1 Scores.

Even though it can be quite normal for gradient boosting to be able to learn imbalanced data well, there could still be biases towards predicting the majority class. We can further improve the model using other complex ways such as SMOTE (which oversamples the minority class), or using other gradient boosting models such as XGBoost or LightBGM, however these models are not under the Sklearn library, hence I will not be using them.

In conclusion, based on my results and reliability tests, Gradient Boosting is the most suitable model for this according to the hyperparameters I have tested it with.

QNAÂ¶

âœ”ï¸ How is your prediction task defined? And what is the meaning of the output variable?


âžœ The task is a classification task to predict Machine Status (this is the target) by learning the contributing factors (used as features)
âžœ The output of 0 means the Machine is Normal and working as per usual, and 1 means the Machine is faulty and working abnormally.

âœ”ï¸Did you process the features in any way?


âžœ Yes, as there are missing values, I have processed them in two ways, both by imputing missing data using the most frequent datas, as well as dropping the rows with missing values, and then comparing to see which produced better results.

I then also preprocessed them using StandardScaler() and OneHotEncoder before training the models with them to improve model performance and prevent feature dominance.

I have also refrained from feature engineering new features as we have a very imbalanced data. Featuring new features can cause the majority data to be even more dominant, and under-represent the minority data even more. Hence to be safe, I have chose not to feature engineer new features

âœ”ï¸ How did you select which learning algorithms to use?


âžœ Since this is a classification problem, I have selected models that are made for classification problems. Then determine which is the best model from the results and checks I have done.

âœ”ï¸ Did you try to tune the hyperparameters of the learning algorithm, and how?


âžœ Yes, I used hyperparameters to set the complexity of the learning algorithms. Since this dataset has severe issue of class imbalance (there are way more 0's than 1's), I have leaned more towards underfitting, to make sure the model is more accurate towards minor changes that could cause Machine Status (The target variable) to be '1'. Reason for this is because, for imbalanced datasets with suspiciously high accuracy, underfitting is safer because it reduces over-reliance on the majority class.

âœ”ï¸ How do you evaluate the quality of your machine learning models?


âžœ I first test their test accuracy to make sure it looks normal (for a dataset with ~96% '0's, the accuracy should not steer too far away from this accuracy). Then evaluate their F1 Score, since it's an imbalanced dataset, we'd need metrics other than test accuracy to determine its quality. I then conduct other tests, such as using an ROC curve to determine overfitting, and then using a checker which compares train accuracy with test accuracy to check for overfitting or underfitting. Then finally using a bar graph comparison to see which model performs best and is most suited for this project

âœ”ï¸ How well does your system compare to a dummy baseline?


âžœ According to my bar graphs and ROC curve comparisons, my actual models perform significantly better than the dummy baseline model. The baseline model is essentially guessing the results, while my actual models proved to be significantly more reliable than the dummy baseline model. Hence, my system is much better compared to a dummy baseline.

âœ”ï¸ Is it possible to say something about which features the model considers important? (Whether this is possible depends on the type of classifier you are using)


âžœ Yes, under Phase 3, I have set up to show bargraphs showing the models which support it (models which have feature_importances_). They show what each model considers is important.
